changing-multiple-columns.label: Changing multiple columns
changing-multiple-columns.summary: Learn how to update more than one column at a time in a SQL UPDATE statement, using the inventory_items table as an example.

q4-multi-choice-when-update-multi-column.title: When should you update multiple columns at once?
q4-multi-choice-when-update-multi-column.prompt: Which of the following are good reasons to update multiple columns in a single UPDATE statement? Select all that apply.
q4-multi-choice-when-update-multi-column.hint: Think about efficiency and data consistency.

help.concept: Updating multiple columns at once is useful when you want to keep related data changes together and reduce the number of queries.
help.hint_1: Consider if the changes are related or need to happen together.
help.hint_2: Also think about minimizing the number of database operations.

q5-fill-blank-choice-keyword.title: Keyword for updating multiple columns
q5-fill-blank-choice-keyword.prompt: Which SQL keyword is used to assign new values to columns in an UPDATE statement?
q5-fill-blank-choice-keyword.hint: It's the word that comes after UPDATE and before the column assignments.

help.concept: The the missing term keyword is used in SQL to specify which columns should be updated and their new values.
help.hint_2: It comes right before listing the columns and values to update.

q5-fill-blank-choice-keyword.template: UPDATE inventory_items ___ status = 'active', last_updated = '2026-02-10' WHERE id = 1;

sketch-1.title: Updating Multiple Columns at Once
sketch-1.bodyMarkdown:
You can update several columns in a single SQL UPDATE statement by separating each column assignment with a comma. For example, suppose you want to set both the `status` and `last_updated` columns for all draft items in the `inventory_items` table:

```sql
UPDATE inventory_items
SET status = 'active', last_updated = '2026-02-10'
WHERE status = 'draft';
```

This statement changes the `status` and `last_updated` fields for every row where `status` is currently `'draft'`. Always use a WHERE clause to avoid updating every row by accident.

sketch-2.title: Preview Before Updating
sketch-2.bodyMarkdown:
Before running an UPDATE that changes multiple columns, it's a good habit to preview which rows will be affected. You can do this with a SELECT statement using the same WHERE condition:

```sql
SELECT id, name, status, last_updated
FROM inventory_items
WHERE status = 'draft';
```

This helps you confirm that your UPDATE will only affect the intended rows.
